subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 18! In this unit you will learn how to describe exactly where things are and how they move. Every day you already do this without thinking. When Amina tells Yusuf that the well is 'two streets north, then one street east', she is describing a position and a movement. When Hassan follows a map from Hargeisa to the market, he is using directions. When Khadija folds a paper to make a matching pattern for Eid, she is using a reflection. In this unit you will learn to do all of these things carefully and mathematically.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Use the eight compass directions (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) to describe direction and movement.
Use coordinates to describe the position of a point in the first quadrant.
Plot points on a coordinate grid and read the coordinates of a marked point.
Describe a movement on a grid using coordinates and directions.
Reflect 2D shapes across vertical and horizontal mirror lines.
Identify the properties of reflected shapes (same size, same shape, equal distances).
Find the coordinates of a reflected point using a mirror line.
Apply position, direction and movement to solve real-world problems and read simple maps.

concepts.title: The Eight Compass Directions
concepts.explanation: A compass is a tool that helps us find our way by showing directions. It has four main directions. Picture a map spread out on the table in front of you: North (N) points toward the top of the map.
concepts.example: East (E) points toward the right of the map.

concepts.title: Describing Movement with Directions
concepts.explanation: Compass directions become truly useful when we combine them with a distance to describe movement from one place to another. A direction on its own tells you which way to face; a distance tells you how far to go. Together they give a complete instruction. For example, 'Walk 3 blocks North' means face the top of the map and travel three blocks that way. 'Travel 2 kilometres East' means face right and go two kilometres. We can chain several instructions together to describe a whole journey. To get from Hassan's home…
concepts.example: When you follow a set of directional instructions, three things matter every time:

concepts.title: Understanding Coordinates
concepts.explanation: Coordinates are a pair of numbers that describe an exact position on a grid. They are far more precise than 'near the top' or 'over on the right'. Coordinates tell us exactly how far to move across and how far to move up from a fixed starting point. Every coordinate grid has two special lines. The x-axis is the horizontal line that runs across, from left to right. The y-axis is the vertical line that runs up and down. The point where the two axes cross is called the origin, and its coordinates are (0, 0) – zero…
concepts.example: A pair of coordinates is always written inside brackets with a comma between the two numbers, like this: (3, 5). The order matters enormously:

concepts.title: Plotting and Reading Coordinates
concepts.explanation: Plotting a point means marking it on the grid when you are given its coordinates. Follow these steps every time to plot the point (x, y): Move x units across (to the right for a positive x).
concepts.example: Move y units up (upwards for a positive y).

concepts.title: Understanding Reflections
concepts.explanation: A reflection is a type of transformation – a way of moving a shape to a new place. When you reflect a shape, you flip it over a straight line called the mirror line (or line of reflection). The new shape you get is the mirror image of the first. You already know what a reflection looks like. When you stand in front of a mirror, the mirror shows an image that looks just like you but flipped: if you raise your right hand, your reflection seems to raise its left. This everyday experience is exactly what happens in…
concepts.example: Reflections always obey four important properties. These are true for every reflection, no matter the shape or the mirror line:

concepts.title: Reflecting Shapes Across a Mirror Line
concepts.explanation: A vertical mirror line runs straight up and down, like a standing pole. A horizontal mirror line runs from side to side, like the horizon. In both cases the method for reflecting a shape is the same, and squared paper makes it much easier. To reflect a shape across a mirror line, work one vertex (corner) at a time:
concepts.example: Count the number of squares from a vertex straight to the mirror line. Count along a line that meets the mirror at a right angle.

methods.title: How to plot a point (x, y)
methods.example: Amina walks from her home to the well in the direction North-West. In which direction must she walk to come straight back home?
steps: Start at the origin (0, 0).
Move x units across to the right.
Move y units up.
Mark and label the point.

methods.title: How to reflect a shape
methods.example: Yusuf starts at a square and moves: 2 squares North, 3 squares East, 1 square South, 1 square West. Describe his overall movement in a single step.
steps: Count the squares from each vertex straight to the mirror line.
Count the same number of squares on the opposite side.
Mark each reflected vertex.
Join the vertices, then fold along the line to check.

methods.title: How to reflect a point with coordinates
methods.example: Plot the point A (5, 3) on a coordinate grid.
steps: Across a vertical line x = a: keep y, and new x = 2a − x.
Across a horizontal line y = b: keep x, and new y = 2b − y.
Check by counting the distance to the line on both sides.

methods.title: Read a point's coordinates
methods.example: A point B is 2 units to the right of the origin and 6 units up. Write its coordinates.
steps: Solution: The across-distance (x) is 2, and the up-distance (y) is 6.
Writing across first and up second gives B (2, 6).
Notice B lies higher up than it does across, because its y-value is larger than its x-value.

methods.title: Describe a movement using coordinates
methods.example: Farah starts at (2, 3). She moves 3 units right and 2 units up. What are her new coordinates?
steps: Solution: Moving right adds to the x-coordinate: 2 + 3 = 5.
Moving up adds to the y-coordinate: 3 + 2 = 5.
So her new position is (5, 5).
Moving right or up always increases the matching coordinate; moving left or down would decrease it.

methods.title: Reflect a point across a vertical line
methods.example: Reflect the point (3, 4) across the vertical mirror line x = 5.
steps: Solution: The height stays the same, so the y-coordinate is still 4.
Use new x = 2a − x with a = 5: new x = 2 × 5 − 3 = 10 − 3 = 7.
The reflection is (7, 4).
Check by counting: (3, 4) is 2 squares left of the line x = 5, and (7, 4) is 2 squares right – equal distances, so it is correct.

workedExamples.title: Name the direction and its opposite
workedExamples.prompt: Amina walks from her home to the well in the direction North-West. In which direction must she walk to come straight back home?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Opposite directions are exact reverses. The opposite of North-West is South-East (SE). So to return home she walks South-East. A quick check: North is opposite South, and West is opposite East, so NW must be opposite SE.

workedExamples.title: Follow a movement on a grid
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf starts at a square and moves: 2 squares North, 3 squares East, 1 square South, 1 square West. Describe his overall movement in a single step.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Combine the opposite directions. Up and down: 2 North minus 1 South leaves 1 square North. Left and right: 3 East minus 1 West leaves 2 squares East. So his whole journey is the same as a single move of 1 square North and 2 squares East.

workedExamples.title: Plot a point
workedExamples.prompt: Plot the point A (5, 3) on a coordinate grid.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Start at the origin (0, 0). Move 5 units right along the x-axis (across the corridor). Then move 3 units up along the y-axis (up the stairs). Mark and label the point A. Because both numbers are positive, A sits comfortably inside the first quadrant, up and to the right of the origin.

workedExamples.title: Read a point's coordinates
workedExamples.prompt: A point B is 2 units to the right of the origin and 6 units up. Write its coordinates.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The across-distance (x) is 2, and the up-distance (y) is 6. Writing across first and up second gives B (2, 6). Notice B lies higher up than it does across, because its y-value is larger than its x-value.

workedExamples.title: Describe a movement using coordinates
workedExamples.prompt: Farah starts at (2, 3). She moves 3 units right and 2 units up. What are her new coordinates?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Moving right adds to the x-coordinate: 2 + 3 = 5. Moving up adds to the y-coordinate: 3 + 2 = 5. So her new position is (5, 5). Moving right or up always increases the matching coordinate; moving left or down would decrease it.

workedExamples.title: Reflect a point across a vertical line
workedExamples.prompt: Reflect the point (3, 4) across the vertical mirror line x = 5.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The height stays the same, so the y-coordinate is still 4. Use new x = 2a − x with a = 5: new x = 2 × 5 − 3 = 10 − 3 = 7. The reflection is (7, 4). Check by counting: (3, 4) is 2 squares left of the line x = 5, and (7, 4) is 2 squares right – equal distances, so it is correct.

workedExamples.title: Reflect a point across a horizontal line
workedExamples.prompt: Reflect the point (2, 6) across the horizontal mirror line y = 3.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The across-value stays the same, so the x-coordinate is still 2. Use new y = 2b − y with b = 3: new y = 2 × 3 − 6 = 6 − 6 = 0. The reflection is (2, 0). Check by counting: (2, 6) is 3 squares above the line y = 3, and (2, 0) is 3 squares below – equal distances, so it is correct.

workedExamples.title: Properties of a reflected shape
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan reflects a rectangle across a mirror line. Its original area is 12 squares and its perimeter is 14 units. What are the area and perimeter of the reflection?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A reflection keeps the shape exactly the same size and shape. So the reflected rectangle also has an area of 12 squares and a perimeter of 14 units. Only the orientation is reversed – the measurements never change.

workedExamples.title: Name the opposite of each direction
workedExamples.prompt: Name the opposite of each direction: a) North b) South-West c) East d) North-West.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: What do all points of the form (4, n) have in common? What about all points of the form (n, 3)?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Follow the directions from square X: 2 North, 1 East, 3 South, 1 West. Describe the overall movement in one step.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Reflect each point across the vertical line x = 5: a) (3, 4) b) (7, 1) c) (0, 3).

activities.title: Make a Compass Rose
steps: Draw a large plus sign, then add diagonal lines to make an eight-armed star.
Label the four main directions: N at the top, E on the right, S at the bottom, W on the left.
Label the four in-between directions: NE, SE, SW, NW.
Stand up, face what you decide is North, and point to each direction as you name it clockwise.
Keep your compass rose – you can use it all through this unit whenever you need to check a direction.

activities.title: Coordinate Treasure Hunt
steps: Draw a grid with an x-axis and a y-axis, both numbered from 0 to 10.
Secretly mark 'treasure' at a point, such as (6, 4), and write down its coordinates.
Give a family member clues like 'go 6 across and 4 up' and let them plot the point.
Swap roles. Then try describing the route to the treasure using compass directions.

activities.title: Grid Reference Battleship
activities.materials: two matching squared grids, a pencil for each player.
steps: Each player secretly marks three small 'boats' on their own grid at chosen coordinates.
Take turns to call out a coordinate, such as (3, 5), to try to hit a boat.
Your opponent says 'hit' or 'miss'. Keep a record of the coordinates you have tried.
The first player to find all of the opponent's boats wins. This game makes reading coordinates fast and accurate.

activities.title: Fold-and-Reflect Patterns
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, scissors (with an adult's help).
steps: Fold a sheet of paper in half to make a crease – this crease is your mirror line.
Draw half a shape or pattern against the fold, then cut around it while the paper is still folded.

activities.title: Direction Investigation Around Your Home
activities.materials: your compass rose from Activity 1, a notebook.
steps: Choose a fixed spot to stand, such as your front door, and decide which way is North.
Look around and record which direction each place is in: the masjid, a neighbour's house, a tree, the road.
Write instructions to walk from your door to one of these places using directions and steps.
Ask a family member to follow your instructions exactly. Did they arrive at the right place?

activities.title: Symmetry Hunt (Family Investigation)
activities.materials: your eyes, and a notebook to record what you find.
steps: Walk around your home and neighbourhood looking for reflective symmetry.
Look at leaves, patterned cloth, woven mats, doorways, and even letters of the alphabet.
For each object, imagine the mirror line and check if both sides really match.
Draw your three favourite symmetrical patterns and mark the mirror line on each.

rules.title: Coordinate Rule
rules.text: Coordinates are written (x, y): across first, then up. Along the corridor, then up the stairs. The origin is (0, 0).

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: Compass Directions Clockwise from the top: N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW. Opposites: N-S, E-W, NE-SW, NW-SE. Say the N or S part first (NE, not EN).

rules.title: Reflection Rule
rules.text: A reflection keeps the same size and shape – only the orientation flips. Every point and its reflection are the same distance from the mirror line, on opposite sides.

terms: A pair of numbers naming a position (x, y)
The horizontal / vertical line on a grid
A star showing all eight directions
A flip of a shape over a mirror line
The line a shape is reflected across
A corner point of a shape
commonMistakes: Across first, then up: 3 across, 5 up
Reflection keeps the same size and shape
Say the N or S part first: NE, SW
Counting the mirror at a slant
Count straight to the line at a right angle

realProblems.prompt: A map of Mombasa uses coordinates. The harbour is at (2, 3) and follows these directions: 3 units East, 2 units North, 1 unit West, 4 units North. What are the end coordinates?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: On a map, the school is at (3, 6) and the well is at (3, 1). In which direction, and how far, do you travel from the school to the well?

realProblems.prompt: A logo is symmetrical about the vertical line x = 3. A star sits at (1, 5) on the left half. Where is the matching star on the right half?

realProblems.prompt: A map scale shows 1 cm for 4 km. Two villages near Garissa are 6 cm apart on the map. What is the real distance between them?
realProblems.answer: South, 5 units (3 to 3 across, 6 to 1 down).

selfAssessment: I can use the eight compass directions (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) to describe direction and movement.
I can use coordinates to describe the position of a point in the first quadrant.
I can plot points on a coordinate grid and read the coordinates of a marked point.
I can describe a movement on a grid using coordinates and directions.
I can reflect 2D shapes across vertical and horizontal mirror lines.
I can identify the properties of reflected shapes (same size, same shape, equal distances).
I can find the coordinates of a reflected point using a mirror line.
I can apply position, direction and movement to solve real-world problems and read simple maps.
